1: A shadow: 'The scatterer has come up against you. Man the ramparts; watch the road; dress for battle; collect all your strength.' The military oracle against Nineveh — the call to prepare for siege that is already futile — sets up the divine pattern of the overthrow of earthly power. Christ's victory over the principalities and powers (Colossians 2:15: he disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, triumphing over them) is the NT's form of the same divine military action — not against a city but against the spiritual forces that hold humanity captive. No human rampart or strength can withstand the divine warrior who comes against them.
2: A revelation of God: 'For the LORD is restoring the majesty of Jacob as the majesty of Israel, for plunderers have plundered them and ruined their branches.' The destruction of Nineveh is not merely punishment of a wicked city but the restoration of YHWH's people — the divine warrior acts against the enemy in order to restore the dignity and flourishing of his covenant people. Christ's victory against sin and death is the same structure: he did not merely defeat evil in the abstract but specifically for the restoration of his people — “I came that they may have life and have it abundantly” (John 10:10). The majesty of Jacob being restored points to the NT's “heirs of God and fellow heirs with Christ” (Romans 8:17) — the plundered dignity of humanity restored in the new creation.
3: A shadow: 'The shield of his mighty men is red; his soldiers are clothed in scarlet. The chariots come with flashing metal on the day he musters them; the cypress spears are brandished.' The vision of the attacking army with red shields and scarlet — the color of battle and blood — is the OT military imagery that Revelation takes up for the returning Christ: “He is clothed in a robe dipped in blood, and the name by which he is called is The Word of God. And the armies of heaven, arrayed in fine linen, white and pure, were following him” (Revelation 19:13-14). The military imagery shifts from the scarlet of human blood-warfare to the white of divine purity — yet the one who leads them wears blood on his robe, the blood of the cross that secured the victory.
4: A theme: 'The chariots race madly through the streets; they rush to and fro through the squares; their appearance is like torches; they dart like lightning.' The chaotic rush of the invading chariots — the city in panic, the ordered streets turned to frantic disorder — is the OT picture of the Day of the LORD's sudden disruption of false security. Paul draws on the same theme: “While people are saying, There is peace and security, then sudden destruction will come upon them as labor pains come upon a pregnant woman, and they will not escape” (1 Thessalonians 5:3). The city that seemed impregnable — Nineveh was surrounded by massive walls — is suddenly overwhelmed. The NT warns against the same false security before Christ's return.
5: A shadow: 'He summons his officers; they stumble as they go; they hasten to the wall; the siege tower is set up.' The frantic mobilization of Nineveh's defenders — stumbling, rushing, setting up defenses — against an unstoppable attacker is the OT pattern for every human resistance to divine judgment. At the Last Day, all human resistance collapses before Christ: “Then will appear in heaven the sign of the Son of Man, and then all the tribes of the earth will mourn, and they will see the Son of Man coming on the clouds of heaven with power and great glory” (Matthew 24:30). No siege tower, no wall, no officer can withstand the divine King when he comes in his glory.
6: A shadow: 'The river gates are opened; the palace melts away.' The opening of Nineveh's river gates — historically fulfilled when the Tigris flooded and undermined the walls in 612 BCE — is the miraculous-natural pattern of YHWH defeating the humanly impregnable. The NT's equivalent is the resurrection: the sealed tomb, the Roman guard, the stone — all the defenses set against Christ's body — are “opened” by the divine power that no human security can contain (Matthew 28:2: there was a great earthquake, for an angel of the Lord descended from heaven and came and rolled back the stone). The palace that melts before divine action is every human certainty that assumes permanence apart from God.
7: A shadow: 'It is decreed that the city be exiled, its slave women led away, moaning like doves and beating their breasts.' The mourning of Nineveh's captive women — formerly the victorious empire, now weeping in exile — is the reversal the Magnificat celebrates (Luke 1:52: he has brought down the mighty from their thrones and exalted those of humble estate). The great reversal that Nahum records historically against Nineveh is the pattern of the final reversal Christ executes: those who exalted themselves will be humbled; those who humbled themselves will be exalted (Matthew 23:12). Nineveh's mourning in exile is the preview of every proud power that will mourn at the Lamb's victory (Revelation 18:9-11).
8: A shadow: ‘Nineveh is like a pool whose waters run away. “Halt! Halt!” they cry, but none turns back.’ The city that seemed like an inexhaustible pool — vast population, resources, military power — drains away when YHWH acts against it. The NT's equivalent is the emptying of all human strength before the cross and resurrection: “For consider your calling, brothers: not many of you were wise according to worldly standards, not many were powerful, not many were of noble birth. But God chose what is foolish in the world to shame the wise; God chose what is weak in the world to shame the strong” (1 Corinthians 1:26-27). The power that seemed permanent drains away; the weakness of the cross turns out to be the power of God.
9: A shadow: 'Plunder the silver, plunder the gold! There is no end of the treasure, or of the wealth of all precious things.' The looting of Nineveh's vast wealth — accumulated through a century of imperial conquest — reverses the plundering of the nations Assyria had done. The ultimate reversal of imperial wealth-extraction is the new creation: “the kings of the earth will bring their glory into it” (Revelation 21:24); “the wealth of the nations will come to you” (Isaiah 60:5). Christ is the one in whom “are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge” (Colossians 2:3) — the true wealth of the new creation that replaces the plundered gold of earthly empires.
10: A shadow: 'Desolation and ruin! Hearts melt and knees tremble; anguish is in all loins; all faces grow pale.' The collapse of Nineveh — the panic, trembling, and anguish — is the OT's physical description of what it is to be on the wrong side of divine judgment. The NT's ultimate form: “Then the kings of the earth and the great ones and the generals and the rich and the powerful, and everyone, slave and free, hid themselves in the caves and among the rocks of the mountains, calling to the mountains and rocks, Fall on us and hide us from the face of him who is seated on the throne, and from the wrath of the Lamb” (Revelation 6:15-16). Nineveh's collapse is the type; the eschatological trembling before the returning Christ is the antitype.
11: A shadow: 'Where is the lions’ den, the feeding place of the young lions, where the lion and lioness went, where his cubs were, with none to disturb?' The taunt against Nineveh — where is the lion (Assyria's royal symbol) now? — is the OT pattern for the complete undoing of predatory power. The NT's answer to this taunt for every predatory power is Christ's resurrection: the one who came “like a roaring lion, seeking someone to devour” (1 Peter 5:8, describing the devil) is ultimately destroyed by the Lion of Judah (Revelation 5:5) who also is the Lamb who was slain. The predator is destroyed by the one who submitted to being prey.
12: A shadow: 'The lion tore enough for his cubs and strangled prey for his lionesses; he filled his caves with prey and his dens with torn flesh.' The image of Assyria as the lion who tore prey for his young — feeding his empire through conquest and plunder — is the OT picture of predatory imperial power. The NT identifies the spiritual power behind such empires: “your adversary the devil prowls around like a roaring lion, seeking someone to devour” (1 Peter 5:8). But Christ came to destroy the works of the devil (1 John 3:8) — the Lion of Judah renders the predatory lion powerless, not by being a greater predator but by being the sacrificial Lamb whose blood purchases the freedom of the prey (Revelation 5:5-9).
13: A revelation of God: '“Behold, I am against you,” declares the LORD of hosts, and I will burn your chariots in smoke, and the sword shall devour your young lions. I will cut off your prey from the earth, and the voice of your messengers shall no longer be heard.' The divine declaration “I am against you” (hineni elayich) — addressed to Nineveh, also to Tyre (Ezekiel 26:3) and Gog (Ezekiel 38:3) — is the most terrible thing YHWH can say to any power. Its NT inversion is the gospel's most glorious announcement: “If God is for us, who can be against us?” (Romans 8:31). The same God who says “I am against you” to every anti-God power says “I am for you” to those in Christ. The cross is the hinge: God's “against” fell on Christ so that his “for us” could be extended to all who trust him.
